name: MLB Steroid Era
description: The MLB Steroid Era refers to a period in Major League Baseball history, roughly from the late 1980s to the mid-2000s, characterized by a perceived increase in the use of performance-enhancing drugs (PEDs), primarily anabolic steroids, by players. Proponents argue that the era fundamentally altered the sport, inflating offensive statistics (home runs, RBIs) and shattering records previously thought unbreakable. They claim that MLB officials knowingly turned a blind eye to the issue, motivated by increased revenue generated by the offensive surge. Evidence cited includes the dramatic increase in home run totals, the physical transformations of certain players, and anecdotal accounts from players and coaches. This perspective often suggests a widespread conspiracy of silence and complicity within the league. The mainstream scholarly perspective acknowledges the widespread use of PEDs during this period, evidenced by positive drug tests, congressional hearings, and admissions from players. However, it avoids characterizing it as a formal conspiracy, attributing it instead to a confluence of factors including: a lack of robust testing protocols, a culture of winning at all costs, and the absence of clear ethical guidelines. Scholars emphasize that attributing inflated statistics *solely* to PEDs is an oversimplification, as advancements in training techniques, improved nutrition, and smaller ballparks also contributed to the offensive environment. Furthermore, the mainstream perspective highlights the significant efforts MLB eventually undertook to combat PED use, including the implementation of stricter testing and penalties.
subtitle: Era of inflated stats and controversy

claims.claim: MLB knowingly ignored steroid use for profit.
claims.evidence: Increased revenue during the era; slow initial response to the problem.
claims.criticism: Lack of direct evidence of explicit collusion; initial lack of awareness & evolving understanding of PEDs.

claims.claim: Steroids were solely responsible for the offensive boom.
claims.evidence: Dramatic increase in home run numbers; physical transformations of players.
claims.criticism: Advances in training, nutrition, and ballpark dimensions also contributed.

claims.claim: Records set during the era are illegitimate and should be asterisked.
claims.evidence: Use of PEDs taints the integrity of the game and its historical record.
claims.criticism: Difficulty in definitively quantifying the impact of PEDs on individual performance; subjective assessment of legitimacy.

keyFigures.name: Jose Canseco
keyFigures.role: One of the first prominent players to publicly admit steroid use and expose its prevalence.

keyFigures.name: Bud Selig
keyFigures.role: MLB Commissioner during much of the era, criticized for the league's slow response.

keyFigures.name: George Mitchell
keyFigures.role: Led the Mitchell Report, an independent investigation into steroid use in MLB.

mythologyConnections: The idea of superhuman strength and endurance, akin to mythological heroes.
The fall from grace due to hubris and the pursuit of excessive power.

timeline.event: Anabolic steroids begin to appear in baseball.
Mark McGwire and Sammy Sosa home run chase captivates the nation.
BALCO scandal exposes a widespread steroid distribution network.
Release of the Mitchell Report details steroid use among numerous players.
Ongoing debate about the legacy of the era and the legitimacy of records.

sources.author: Jose Canseco
sources.work: Juiced: Wild Times, Rampant 'Roids, Smash Hits & How Baseball Got Big
sources.title: Jose Canseco

sources.author: Selig, Bud
sources.work: For the Good of the Game: The Inside Story of My Life in Baseball
sources.title: Selig, Bud
